title: Lemon Lentil & Spinach Dahl
description: Reheat: stovetop with a splash of water. Excellent for lunch leftovers.
instructions: Add lentils, coconut milk and water or stock.
Simmer until soft, then stir in spinach and lemon.
baby: Keep spices mild. Mash or blend a portion; serve with rice. Avoid adding salt to the baby portion.
nutrition: Lentils bring steady plant protein, iron and fibre; lemon helps the body absorb more of the iron from the spinach and lentils.
detailedInstructions: Finely chop the onion and garlic. Soften them in olive oil or ghee for 5-6 minutes, then add curry powder, cumin, turmeric or garam masala and cook for 30 seconds until fragrant.
Rinse the red lentils, then add them with coconut milk and enough low-salt stock or water to cover generously. Simmer for 15-20 minutes, stirring often, until the lentils collapse into a creamy dahl.
Stir through spinach at the end so it wilts but stays green. Finish with lemon juice, taste the adult portion for salt, and loosen with hot water if leftovers thicken.
Serve with rice, yoghurt and extra lemon. For Thermomix, chop onion and garlic first, saute with spices, then cook lentils with liquid before stirring in spinach.

title: Chicken, Sweet Potato & Broccoli Traybake
description: Reheat: covered in oven at 170°C. Good Monday anchor meal.
instructions: Roast chicken and sweet potato first.
Add broccoli later so it does not burn.
baby: Offer soft shredded chicken, mashed sweet potato, and very soft broccoli florets. Cut into safe finger-food sizes.
nutrition: Chicken gives complete protein while sweet potato adds slow-release carbs and beta-carotene. Broccoli brings vitamin C and folate.
detailedInstructions: Cut sweet potato into even chunks and roast with olive oil for 15 minutes so it gets a head start.
Add chicken thighs, season the adult portion, and roast until the chicken is cooked through and the potato is soft.
Add broccoli for the final 10-12 minutes so it stays bright rather than scorched. Finish with a quick tahini, lemon and yoghurt sauce.

title: Quinoa Loaded Bowl
description: Tip: keep sauce separate; eat warm or room temperature.
ingredients: 4 tbsp olive oil or yoghurt sauce
baby: Serve deconstructed: soft quinoa, mashed chickpeas, roasted veg strips. Go easy on salty feta.
nutrition: Quinoa, chickpeas or feta, greens and sauce make a balanced bowl with protein, fibre, minerals and enough fat to keep it satisfying.

title: Chickpea Tomato Feta Skillet
description: A strong Thursday meal: warm, fast, and forgiving.
instructions: Serve with rice, potatoes or GF bread.
baby: Squash chickpeas flat to reduce choking risk. Keep feta minimal because it is salty.
nutrition: Chickpeas add fibre and plant protein, tomatoes add vitamin C, and feta gives flavour so the meal feels generous with little effort.

title: Green Power Soup
description: Reheat: Thermomix or stovetop. Add bread, eggs or cheese for extra protein.
instructions: Cook vegetables and beans with stock.
Top with olive oil, seeds or cheese.
baby: Serve thick in a small bowl or on pre-loaded spoons. Add yoghurt to cool it quickly.
nutrition: Beans make this soup more filling, while peas and greens add folate, fibre and vitamin C. Olive oil helps absorb fat-soluble nutrients.

title: Baked Eggs & Greens
description: Best fresh, but sauce base can be made ahead.
instructions: Crack in eggs and bake or cover until set.
baby: Cook eggs fully until firm. Chop spinach finely. Serve with soft toast fingers or potato.
nutrition: Eggs are rich in choline, iodine and protein; greens add folate and magnesium. Serve with a carb to make it a full recovery meal.

title: Thai Curry
description: Make it saucy so leftovers reheat well.
instructions: Simmer curry paste and coconut milk.
baby: Keep baby portion mild before adding extra curry paste or chilli. Serve rice with soft veg and shredded protein.
nutrition: Coconut milk and rice make it comforting, while vegetables plus chicken, tofu or chickpeas cover protein and fibre. Keep plenty of veg in the pan.
detailedInstructions: Fry curry paste briefly in a little oil, then add coconut milk and stir until it becomes a smooth sauce.
Add chicken, tofu or chickpeas with the vegetables that need longest cooking first. Simmer gently until tender.
Finish with lime or lemon and serve over rice. Keep a mild baby portion aside before adding extra paste or chilli.

title: Tacos / Wraps
description: Good Saturday meal. Also useful for using up leftovers.
baby: Serve fillings separately: avocado, beans squashed flat, soft veg, shredded chicken. Avoid hard raw pieces.
nutrition: This is a flexible protein-plus-veg meal: beans add fibre, avocado adds healthy fats, and yoghurt sauce gives protein and calcium.
detailedInstructions: Warm beans, mince or shredded chicken with mild spices and a splash of water so the filling stays soft.
Set out tortillas or wraps with avocado, tomato or salsa, yoghurt sauce and any leftover vegetables.
Let everyone build their own, keeping the baby plate deconstructed with soft fillings and no hard raw pieces.

title: Saucy Gnocchi
description: Key rule: keep it saucy; dry gnocchi is poor without a microwave.
ingredients: 250 g optional sausage, beans or feta
instructions: Heat sauce with greens and protein.
baby: Cut gnocchi into small pieces. Keep sauce mild and not too salty.
nutrition: Gnocchi covers the comfort-carb role. Adding spinach plus sausage, beans or feta turns it from a carb bowl into a more sustaining dinner.

title: 10-minute Egg Fried Rice
description: Keep frozen veg and eggs stocked at all times.
baby: Use low-salt tamari or leave seasoning out of baby portion. Ensure peas/veg are soft; egg fully cooked.
nutrition: Eggs add protein and choline, rice gives quick energy, and frozen veg adds colour and fibre with almost no prep.

title: Soup + Eggs + Bread
description: A realistic fatigue meal. Not glamorous, but solid.
ingredients: 1 litre frozen soup or jarred soup
4 slices GF bread or 600 g potatoes
instructions: Add eggs and bread or potatoes.
baby: Use low-salt soup if possible. Serve thick soup with fully cooked egg and soft bread fingers.
nutrition: Eggs upgrade a light soup with protein and micronutrients. Add olive oil or cheese when everyone needs more staying power.

title: Yoghurt Bowl Dinner
description: Best as an emergency adult meal, not the main family default.
instructions: Add extra nuts/seeds for staying power.
baby: Use plain full-fat yoghurt. Avoid whole nuts; use smooth nut butter or ground seeds.
nutrition: Greek yoghurt gives protein and calcium; nut butter or ground seeds add fats and minerals. Fruit makes it fresh without cooking.

title: Turkey or Lentil Bolognese
description: Reheat: stovetop with splash water. Make double and freeze sauce.
ingredients: 500 g turkey mince or 250 g red lentils
instructions: Cook the veg base slowly if you can.
Add mince or lentils and tomatoes.
baby: Keep baby portion low salt. Serve sauce with small pasta, potato, or soft veg; mince should be finely broken up.
nutrition: Turkey brings lean protein and zinc; lentils add iron and fibre. The veg base quietly increases colour and micronutrients.

title: Moroccan Chickpea & Carrot Stew
description: Reheat: stovetop. Add yoghurt or feta for protein and richness.
baby: Keep spices gentle. Squash chickpeas and make carrots very soft.
nutrition: Chickpeas and carrots make this fibre-rich and gentle. Yoghurt or feta adds protein and fat for a more complete bowl.

title: Salmon Potato Traybake
description: Best fresh. Leftovers work cold/room temp as a lunch bowl.
instructions: Add salmon and greens for the last 12–15 min.
Finish with lemon and olive oil.
baby: Check carefully for bones. Flake salmon; serve with mashed potato and soft green beans.
nutrition: Salmon brings omega-3 fats, iodine and protein; potatoes provide gentle carbs and greens add folate and vitamin C.

title: Red Pesto Beans on Toast
description: Very good Thursday option: minimal chopping, high satisfaction.
instructions: Warm beans with pesto and splash water.
baby: Use pesto sparingly because it can be salty. Squash beans and serve on soft toast strips.
nutrition: White beans are the quiet hero here: fibre, plant protein and minerals. Spinach and cheese round it out quickly.

title: Omelette Plate
description: Not fancy, but ideal when the household is tired.
instructions: Serve with raw veg and a carb.
baby: Cook egg through. Cut into strips; serve with soft avocado or tomato pieces.
nutrition: Eggs are nutrient-dense and fast, with protein, choline and iodine. Add avocado, potatoes or bread so it feels like dinner.

title: Coconut Noodle Soup
description: Make only what you need; noodles are less good as leftovers.
instructions: Add rice noodles at the end.
baby: Cut noodles short. Keep broth low salt and temperature safe; offer soft veg/protein pieces.
nutrition: A soothing bowl with protein from tofu, chicken or prawns. Frozen veg keeps it colourful and rice noodles make it easy to eat.

title: Baked Potato Bar
description: Hands-off and child-friendly. Good weekend option.
baby: Serve mashed potato with yoghurt, soft beans, or cheese in small amounts. Avoid tough potato skin.
nutrition: Potatoes are filling and rich in potassium. Beans, tuna, cheese or yoghurt turn them into a proper balanced meal.

title: Frittata with Potatoes & Greens
description: Good warm or cold. Useful for lunches without reheating.
instructions: Warm potatoes and veg in pan.
Finish under grill or in oven.
baby: Cook egg fully. Cut into soft strips or cubes; chop greens finely.
nutrition: Eggs and potatoes make this satisfying; greens add folate and fibre. It is especially useful cold for lunch.

title: Halloumi Veg Bowls
description: Best fresh, but components work well for lunch bowls.
baby: Halloumi is salty, so offer only a little. Focus on soft veg, hummus and grains.
nutrition: Halloumi gives quick protein and satisfaction, while grains, veg and hummus add fibre, colour and slower energy.

title: Rotisserie Chicken Plate
description: Good to keep in mind for genuinely depleted days.
ingredients: 4 rice cakes, GF bread slices or potatoes
baby: Check salt level and remove skin. Shred chicken finely; serve with avocado and soft carb.
nutrition: A good no-cook protein anchor. Add avocado or olive oil plus a carb so it is restorative rather than just snacky.

title: Tuna, Beans & Tomato Salad
description: High protein, high convenience. Not toddler-first, but excellent adult fallback.
instructions: Serve with bread, potatoes or rice cakes.
baby: Use tuna occasionally and choose low-salt if possible. Squash beans and cut tomato small.
nutrition: Tuna adds protein and omega-3s; beans bring fibre and minerals. Tomato and lemon brighten it and add vitamin C.

title: Freezer Soup Upgrade
description: Turns a light soup into a proper dinner.
instructions: Top with oil, seeds or cheese.
baby: Keep baby portion low salt. Blend beans/lentils into soup or mash well.
nutrition: Beans or lentils turn soup into a more sustaining meal, adding protein, fibre and iron without much extra effort.

title: Golden Chicken, Rice & Greens Pot
description: A gentle all-in-one meal for tired weeks: protein, carbs, greens and enough softness for leftovers.
instructions: Soften garlic, ginger and carrots with turmeric.
Add rice, chicken and stock; simmer until rice is tender.
Shred chicken, stir in greens, and serve with yoghurt.
baby: Keep stock low salt. Shred chicken finely, mash carrots, and chop greens small.
nutrition: Chicken, rice and greens give protein, gentle carbs and folate in one pot. Ginger and turmeric keep it warming without needing heat.

title: Beef, Lentil & Hidden Veg Ragu
description: Make a double batch. It freezes well and brings iron, zinc and fibre without feeling worthy.
instructions: Cook onion, grated carrot and courgette until soft.
Add beef, lentils, passata and herbs.
Simmer until rich; loosen with water if needed.
baby: Break mince up finely and keep salt out. Serve with small pasta, potato, polenta or soft veg.
nutrition: Beef is rich in bioavailable iron and B12; lentils and hidden veg add fibre so the sauce is hearty and steadying.
detailedInstructions: Cook onion, carrot and courgette slowly until soft and sweet. Add garlic and herbs for the final minute.
Brown the beef, breaking it up finely, then stir in red lentils and passata with enough water to keep it saucy.
Simmer for 25-30 minutes until the lentils disappear into the sauce. Serve with pasta, potatoes or polenta and freeze extra portions.

title: Thermomix Carrot, Ginger & Red Lentil Soup
description: Good as dinner with eggs, toast or cheese; excellent as a lunch thermos the next day.
instructions: Chop carrot and ginger, then saute briefly.
Add lentils, stock and coconut milk; cook until soft.
Blend smooth and finish with citrus.
baby: Keep ginger gentle and stock low salt. Serve thick on pre-loaded spoons with yoghurt to cool.
nutrition: Red lentils add protein and iron, carrots bring beta-carotene, and coconut milk makes it satisfying enough for tired evenings.
detailedInstructions: Chop carrot, onion and ginger, then saute briefly with olive oil until fragrant.
Add rinsed red lentils, stock and coconut milk. Cook until the carrots and lentils are very soft.
Blend until smooth, then finish with lemon or lime. Add yoghurt, eggs or toast alongside if it needs to be a fuller dinner.

title: Sardine Tomato Pasta
description: Fast, mineral-rich and surprisingly satisfying. Use tuna or salmon if sardines are a hard sell.
ingredients: 500 ml passata or 300 g cherry tomatoes
instructions: Cook pasta while garlic and tomatoes simmer.
Finish with lemon and olive oil.
baby: Check carefully for bones and choose low-salt fish if possible. Flake finely and cut pasta small.
nutrition: Sardines are rich in omega-3s, calcium and vitamin D. Tomato and lemon add brightness and help balance the richness.

title: Miso Butter Beans & Greens
description: Creamy, savoury and almost no chopping. Good when you need real food quickly.
instructions: Warm beans with a splash of water.
Stir in miso, butter or oil, and greens.
Serve over rice or toast with lemon.
baby: Miso is salty, so set aside beans before adding it. Squash beans and chop greens finely.
nutrition: Butter beans bring fibre and plant protein; greens add folate and magnesium. Miso gives depth, so keep extra salt low.

title: Rainbow Nourish Plates
description: Very much in the gentle, abundant bowl-meal direction: flexible, colourful and leftover-friendly.
ingredients: 500 g eggs, chicken, tofu or beans
instructions: Put grain, protein, veg and sauce on the table.
Let everyone assemble their own plate.
Keep extra components for lunch bowls.
baby: Serve deconstructed with soft veg, mashed avocado, squashed beans or shredded protein. Avoid whole seeds.
nutrition: This is balanced by design: carb, protein, colour, fat and sauce. The variety helps cover more micronutrients across the week.
detailedInstructions: Start with a cooked grain, then add one protein, two colourful vegetables, one creamy element and one sauce.
Keep the components separate on the table so adults can build bowls and the baby can have a safe deconstructed plate.
Make extra grain, protein and sauce so tomorrow lunch is mostly assembly.

title: Warm Salmon, Pea & Potato Salad
description: Works warm for dinner and cold for lunch, which is useful when reheating is awkward.
instructions: Boil potatoes, adding peas near the end.
Toss with yoghurt, dill, lemon and greens.
baby: Check salmon for bones, flake well, and squash peas. Serve cucumber only if soft or finely chopped.
nutrition: Salmon covers omega-3 fats and protein, peas add fibre, and potatoes make the meal gentle but filling.

title: Hummus Snack-Board Dinner
description: For evenings when cooking would tip the whole day over. Still balanced enough to count.
ingredients: 4 boiled eggs or 400 g cooked chicken
instructions: Put hummus, protein, carbs, fruit and veg on a board or plates.
Add olive oil, lemon or herbs if you have them.
baby: Spread hummus thinly, quarter tomatoes lengthwise, and cut egg or chicken into safe soft pieces.
nutrition: Hummus, eggs or chicken, avocado and fruit make a no-cook plate with protein, fats, fibre and enough freshness to feel alive.
detailedInstructions: Put hummus, eggs or cooked chicken, avocado, fruit, tomatoes and crackers or toast on plates or a board.
Add olive oil, lemon, herbs or seeds for the adults if they are within reach.
For the baby, spread hummus thinly and cut round or firm foods into safe shapes.
